coredata.dc:title: Novel carbon dots from phenylenediamine for simultaneous detection of peroxydisulfate and phosphate with a smart phone by dual-channel of fluorometry and colorimetry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dou
Shao, Tong
Zhang, Likai
Wang, Xiaoshuang
Yue, Qiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), one type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, showed extensive application in food analysis. Herein, CDs as fluorometry and colorimetry probes were developed to determine peroxydisulfate (PDS) and phosphate ion (Pi) in food samples. CDs were developed with one-pot hydrothermal process from 5-amino salicylic acid and o/m-phenylenediamine named o/m-CDs. o-CDs and m-CDs showed bright green fluorescence with quantum yield at 5.73 % and 6.40 %, which was quenched by PDS and Pi. Fluorometry was based on fluorescence quenching with LOD at 1.6 nM (PDS) and 5.2 nM (Pi). The colorimetry was based on color change of CDs from colorless to brown and indigo blue with LOD at 2.4 (PDS) and 21.1 μM (Pi). Interestingly, for both channels there was no interfering of each other. For portable detection, a wechat mini program of smart phone was employed to calculate the color change. Furthermore, the systems were potential for application in food safety analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S2O8 2−
PO4 3−
Carbon dots
Smart phone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dual-mode detection of PDS based on o-CDs
Dual-mode detection of Pi based on m-CDs
Portable detection of PDS and Pi on test strips

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Excitation, emission fluorescence and absorption spectra of (A) o-CDs and (B) m-CDs (Fluorescence was briefly named FL, inset: digital images of o-CDs and m-CDs solution in natural (left) and UV light (right)).
TEM images of (A) o-CDs and (B) m-CDs (inset: the particle size distribution of CDs).
(A) XRD pattern, (B) FT-IR spectra and XPS survey profile of (C) o-CDs and (D) m-CDs.

(A) Effect of anion on the fluorescence intensity of o-CDs, (B) anti-interference of o-CDs for fluorometric detection of PDS, (C) Effect of anion on the absorbance of o-CDs, and (D) anti-interference of o-CDs for colorimetric detection of PDS (inset: digital images of o-CDs solution with and without ions corresponding to the diagram under natural light).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of o-CDs in the presence of PDS with different concentrations, (B) linear relationship between F0 F and Log [PDS], (C) absorption spectra of o-CDs in the presence of PDS with different concentrations, and (D) linear relationship between absorbance and [PDS] (inset: digital images of o-CDs solution with different concentrations of PDS in natural light).

(A) Effect of anion on fluorescence intensity of m-CDs, (B) anti-interference of m-CDs for fluorometric detection of Pi, (C) effect of anion on absorbance of m-CDs, (D) anti-interference of m-CDs for colorimetric detection of Pi (inset: digital images of m-CDs solution with different concentrations of Pi in natural light).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of m-CDs in the presence of Pi with different concentrations, (B) linear relationship between F0 F and [Pi], (C) absorbance of m-CDs in the presence of PDS with different concentrations, (D) linear relationship between absorbance and [Pi] (inset: photos of m-CDs with different concentrations of Pi in day light).
Portable detection of PDS and Pi using smart phone.
Synthesis of CDs and their application in detection of PDS and Pi

head.ce:title: Novel carbon dots from phenylenediamine for simultaneous detection of peroxydisulfate and phosphate with a smart phone by dual-channel of fluorometry and colorimetry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), one type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, showed extensive application in food analysis. Herein, CDs as fluorometry and colorimetry probes were developed to determine peroxydisulfate (PDS) and phosphate ion (Pi) in food samples. CDs were developed with one-pot hydrothermal process from 5-amino salicylic acid and o/m-phenylenediamine named o/m-CDs. o-CDs and m-CDs showed bright green fluorescence with quantum yield at 5.73 % and 6.40 %, which was quenched by PDS and Pi. Fluorometry was based on fluorescence quenching with LOD at 1.6 nM (PDS) and 5.2 nM (Pi). The colorimetry was based on color change of CDs from colorless to brown and indigo blue with LOD at 2.4 (PDS) and 21.1 μM (Pi). Interestingly, for both channels there was no interfering of each other. For portable detection, a wechat mini program of smart phone was employed to calculate the color change. Furthermore, the systems were potential for application in food safety analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Peroxydisulfate (S2O8 2−, PDS) and phosphate ion (PO4 3−, Pi) are two common food additives that frequently appear in food processing and products. PDS possesses a strong oxidation function is frequently utilized in food, chemical, pharmaceutical and cosmetic industries (Li et al., 2023). Excessive utilization of PDS may impose environmental burden (Oliveira et al., 2003; Shimada et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2015) and its excessive exposure can lead to occupational asthma and skin diseases (Chiseop et al., 2017; Xu, Ren, et al., 2019). Pi plays a significant role in numerous life processes including energy storage and cell metabolism (Deng et al., 2023; Panseri et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2011). The excessive Pi, however, may result in the eutrophication of water, leading to adverse effects on fish populations and causing harm to ecosystems (Zhang et al., 2022). The report from European Food Safety Authority and Panel on food additives and flavourings added to food shows the acceptable daily intake of Pi expressed as phosphorus of 40 mg/kg body weight (Younes et al., 2019). Excessive intake of Pi is likely to induce kidney (Rubio-Aliaga, 2020) and heart diseases (Nathania et al., 2018). Thus, it is crucial to detect PDS and Pi in food.

For this purpose, a considerable number of analytical methods have been developed, including ion chromatography (McDowell et al., 2004), spectrophotometry (Miller & Arai, 2016), electrochemical methods (Forano & Farhat, 2018) and fluorometry (Li et al., 2022). These methods exhibit good performance in detection of PDS and Pi. However, most of them are used in lab and can't meet the need of food safety analysis including on-site and real-time detection. Consequently, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered widespread attention in PDS and Pi determination (Yu et al., 2022), due to their excellent optical character, facile to prepare and extensive precursor materials (Gao et al., 2021; Yang, Jin, et al., 2021). Nevertheless, most existing CDs systems rely on single technique such as fluorometry (Xu, Zou, et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Zou et al., 2019) and colorimetry (Li, Chen, et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2022). In addition, most of these approaches for the detection of PDS or Pi were realized based on CDs combined with metal ion or multiple CDs. The single detection method is limited by a narrow detection range, low detection limit and lack of portability. Therefore, the aim of this work is to develop a method for simultaneous detection of PDS and Pi using dual-channel mode with extended detection range, improved sensitivity, as well as portability.

Herein, fluorometric and colorimetric methods were employed to detect PDS and Pi in food samples using CDs as probes with high specificity. Two CDs named o-CDs and m-CDs were synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal approach. 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) and two isomers of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) were employed as carbon and nitrogen sources. The responses of PDS and Pi to the fluorescence and the color of CDs were explored. Interestingly, the green fluorescence of o-CDs and m-CDs was quenched selectively by PDS and Pi, respectively. Moreover, a smart phone application for colorimetric detection was developed to enhance the diversity and completeness of the method. The color change of o-CDs and m-CDs were combined with test paper and smart phone for visible and portable detection of PDS and Pi in real food samples. The preparation and application of CDs were illustrated in Scheme 1 .

UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a UH4150 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were captured with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were acquired using a JEM 2100 electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was recorded using a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected using a D/Max-2500 diffractometer (Rigaku Co., Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA). The result of dynamic light scattering (DLS) were obtained with a Zetasizer Nanozsp (Malvern Panaco., UK). Ion chromatography (IC) (Thermo Fisher., Mexico) was used as a control method for detection of PDS or Pi in real samples. Smart phone (Redmi Note 9, China) and software of colorimetric detector were used to capture digital images and RGB values of samples, respectively.

5-ASA, OPD and MPD were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaBr, NaCl, NaF, NaHSO3, NaI, NaNO2, NaNO3, Na3PO4, Na2S2, Na2S2O3, Na2S2O8, Na2SO3 and Na2SO4 were purchased from Shanghai Macklin biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the chemical reagents were analytical grade and used directly without further purification unless special instruction. The solutions used in the experiment were all prepared with ultrapure water (Table S1, in Supplementary Material).

o/m-CDs were synthesized with reference to the previous method of CDs with some modifications (Yang, Zhang, et al., 2021). 5-ASA (200 mg), OPD (100 mg) and ultrapure water (20 mL) were added to poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined reactor. The solution was heated at 180 °C in the high-pressure reactor for 6 h after mixing evenly under ultrasound for 30 min. After the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged and filtrated using a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large particulate impurities. Finally, o-CDs as products were dialyzed through a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h. One portion was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments, and the other portion was dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C to obtain grayish brown o-CDs solid for characterization and analysis. m-CDs were prepared by the similar method as that of o-CDs, with the difference that OPD was replaced by MPD.

The dispersion of CDs was prepared in ultrapure water. Standard solutions of PDS and Pi were prepared in ultrapure water. The standard curves were plotted based on fluorescence or color change of CDs and purity of PDS and Pi. The specificity of CDs for PDS and Pi detection was also evaluated by comparison of fluorescence intensity or absorbance of CDs with and without other anions. The fluorescence and absorption spectra of CDs were recorded in the range of 400–650 nm, 300–700 nm for PDS and 340–600 nm, 400–750 nm for Pi detection, respectively.

Trisodium phosphate serves as an emulsifier, leavening agent and modifier to enhance flavor and increase the volume of food, which is commonly utilized in the production of noodles, bread, and other food processing applications (Chen et al., 2019). Persulfate functions as a preservative, bleach, and oxidant to prolong the shelf life of foods and improve their appearance (Carlos & De Jager, 2017; Zhang et al., 2024). Therefore, noodle, bread and drinking water were selected as real food samples to confirm the practical application of the present approach for the detection of PDS and Pi. Instant noodles were purchased from local supermarket of Liaocheng city of China. Bread was ordered from online shopping products. Potable water was obtained from a chemical plant in Binzhou City of China. The pretreatment process of real samples was carried out according to previous literature (Song et al., 2020). Briefly, noodle and bread samples were milled into fine powder and subsequently dissolved in ultrapure water. The solution was allowed to stand for 24 h before filtration to obtain a clear solution, which was then stored at 4 °C for further analysis. Water samples were treated with similar precipitation, filtration and storage.

First, the optical properties of o-CDs and m-CDs were studied through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The absorption spectra for o-CDs and m-CDs exhibit two different peaks at 290 nm, 451 nm (Fig. 1A) and 292 nm, 477 nm (Fig. 1B), respectively. The absorption peaks at 290 nm and 292 nm correspond to the transition of π-π* in CC function group (Xu et al., 2020). The peak in the range of 320–500 nm is considered to n-π* transition of CO and CN (Fig. 1) (Architha et al., 2021). There is no discernible disparity between the two absorption peaks and their respective carbon bond transition configurations. The dispersion of o-CDs or m-CDs shows green fluorescence under UV lamp (365 nm) (inset of Fig. 1A and Fig. 1B). The effect of excitation wavelength on the fluorescence spectra of o-CDs and m-CDs was investigated (Fig. S1, in Supplementary Material). It can be found that there is no significant wavelength shift in the emission peak when the excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 360 nm. It indicates that o-CDs and m-CDs possess the property of excitation-independent (Omer et al., 2018). The maximum excitation and emission wavelength is about 350 nm and 530 nm for o-CDs and m-CDs (Fig.1).

Second, the morphology and functional groups of o/m-CDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS. The TEM image and corresponding particle size distribution of CDs were shown in Fig. 2 . The images indicate that CDs exhibit mono dispersion and nearly spherical morphology, with particle size at 1.39 nm and 2.06 nm, respectively (inset of Fig. 2). In addition, o-CDs and m-CDs undergo significant aggregation in the presence of PDS and Pi (Fig. S2, in Supplementary Material).

The crystal structure and functional groups of o/m-CDs were analyzed by XRD (Fig. 3A). The XRD data of CDs show obvious broad peaks at approximately 21°, which corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon (Nuengmatcha et al., 2018). From Fig. 3B, it can be observed that the absorption peaks at 3340 cm−1 and 3280 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of –OH and NH (Wang et al., 2022), respectively. The absorption peak at 3010–2820 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of CH (Li et al., 2021). The peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 1510 cm−1 are stretching vibration of C=O/C=C, respectively (Mondal & Saha, 2019). The peaks at around

1500 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching and bending vibration of NH bond and the bending vibration of CN bond in amide group (Mohandoss et al., 2023). Finally, peaks at 643 cm−1, 887 cm−1, 832 cm−1 and 760 cm−1 correspond to the benzene ring and as well as the adjacent and meta substituted groups (Yuan et al., 2022). By comparison of the functional groups on the surface of o-CDs and m-CDs, there are multiple groups such as OH, NH, CO and CN groups. In addition, for o-CDs and m-CDs, the properties are similar including morphology, size, crystal state and functional groups.

The element composition and functional groups of o-CDs and m-CDs were further confirmed by XPS (Fig. 3C, D). The XPS full spectra display obvious peaks at about 285 eV, 399 eV and 532 eV, which correspond to the binding energy of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Yang, Jin, et al., 2021; Gan et al., 2020; Edison et al., 2016). The content of C, N and O elements for o-CDs and m-CDs was also measured (Table S2, in Supplementary Material). By comparison with m-CDs, it is evident that there are more C element and less N element in the structure of o-CDs, while the O content is similar. Thus, there are more C=C/C-C groups in o-CDs than in m-CDs, which is consistent with the results of FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. S3A and Fig. S4 A (in Supplementary Material), the peaks at about 284.2 eV and 285.6 eV correspond to C=C/C-C, C=O/C-N and CO in the C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of two CDs, respectively (Jia et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2024). N1s spectra can be decomposed into two peaks of pyridine N and pyrrole N (Fig. S3B and Fig. S4B, in Supplementary Material) (Guo et al., 2016; Wen & Fan, 2016). The high-resolution spectra of O1s can be ascribed to -OH and C-O/C-O-C (Fig. S3C and Fig. S4C, in Supplementary Material) (Mohandoss et al., 2023; Nagaraj et al., 2022). The results of XPS are consistent with those of FT-IR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode detection of PDS based on o-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the present method with o-CDs was explored for the detection of PDS (Fig. 4 ). 15 inorganic ions were selected as possible interfering species such as Br−, Cl−, F−, HSO3 −, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −, Pi, S2−, S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, P2O7 4−, HPO4 2− and H2PO4 −. It can be seen that only PDS can cause fluorescence quenching of o-CDs (Fig. 4A), and the fluorescence (FL) intensity of o-CDs in the presence and absence of PDS is almost unaffected by other ions (Fig. 4B). From the digital images, it can be clearly observed that only PDS makes the colorless o-CDs dispersion appear brown even with other ions (inset of Fig. 4C and D). The absorbance of o-CDs at 425 nm also shows the obvious enhancement with PDS rather than any other ions (Fig. 4C and D). Therefore, there is high specificity for both fluorometry and colorimetry determination of PDS based on o-CDs comparing with other methods (Table S3, in Supplementary Material).

The fluorescence spectra of o-CDs were investigated in the wavelength range of 400–650 nm with excitation wavelength of 353 nm. The peak intensity decreases gradually with increasing concentration of PDS (from 0 to 300 μM) (Fig. 5A). The corresponding relationship between FL intensity and concentration of PDS is shown in Fig. 4B, which shows a good linearity. The linear equation is F0-F = 599.33Log[PDS]-31.78 (R2 = 0.9901), and the limit of detection (LOD) is 1.6 nM (Fig. 5B). For the colorimetric channel, the maximum absorbance of o-CDs solution increases gradually at the range of 300–700 nm, as the concentration of PDS changes from 0 to 300 μM (Fig. 5C). The same solution of o-CDs turns from colorless into brown in the presence of PDS and its color deepens with the increasing concentration of PDS (inset of Fig. 5C). There is a linear relationship between absorbance and concentration of PDS (Fig. 5D). The linear equation is A = 0.002[PDS] + 0.1194 (R2 = 0.9961) with LOD at 2.4 μM. By comparison with previous methods, the proposed one owns wider linear range and lower LOD (Table S3, in Supplementary Material). Most importantly, the present approach realizes the portability of analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode detection of Pi based on m-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of dual-channel method based on m-CDs was investigated for Pi detection (Fig. 6 ). 14 anions were tested such as Br−, Cl−, F−, HSO3 −, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −, S2−, S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, P2O7 4−, HPO4 2− and H2PO4 −. It is obviously observed that only Pi causes fluorescence quenching of m-CDs (Fig. 6A), and the quenching effect is almost unaffected by other ions (Fig. 6B). Moreover, the color of m-CDs changes from nearly colorless to indigo blue with Pi, while there is no significant change for other anions (inset of Fig. 6C and D). The absorbance at 360 nm for m-CDs increases from about 0.05 to 0.45 with Pi, while there is negligible change of absorbance for other anions (Fig. 6C and D). Thus, the dual-channel methods show excellent selectivity for Pi sensing by comparison with other methods (Table S4, in Supplementary Material).

From the fluorescence spectra of m-CDs, it can be found that the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs decreases gradually with increasing concentration of Pi in the range of 0–300 μM (Fig. 7A). The corresponding relationship between FL intensity and Pi concentration is in a good linearity (Fig. 7B). Linear regression equation is F0-F = 14.76 [Pi] + 856.0613 (R2 = 0.9943), and the LOD is 5.2 nM. In addition, the absorbance of m-CDs increases gradually with increasing concentration of Pi from 150 μM to 700 μM (Fig. 7C). The corresponding linear relationship between absorbance and Pi concentration is shown in Fig. 7D. The linear equation is A = 9.633 [Pi]-0.2614 (R2 = 0.9926) with LOD at 21.1 μM, which is much lower than that of previous work (Table S4, in Supplementary Material). At the same time, the proposed systems show high sensitivity and excellent portability comparing with previous methods (Table S4, in Supplementary Material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable detection of PDS and Pi on test strips
ce:section.ce:para: o-CDs and m-CDs modified test strips were prepared based on filter paper, which exhibit obvious color change in the linear range of 0–300 μM. In this case, the visual determination of PDS and Pi was realized based on the color change of o-CDs and m-CDs, respectively. At the same time, the color was captured by mobile phone and transferred to RGB value by an applet named Colorimetric Detector. There are good linear relationship between educlidean distance value and Log[PDS] or Log[Pi] in the range of 0–300 μM with LOD at 0.29 μM and 4.75 μM for PDS and Pi detection, respectively (Fig. 8 ). It offers a more expedient approach for the detection of PDS and Pi in comparison to previous methods (Li et al., 2022; Xuan et al., 2022). The present system demonstrates superior effectiveness for sensing of PDS and Pi through a comprehensive comparison with other works from analytical sensitivity, dynamic range, precision, matrix effect and portability (Table S3 and Table S4, in Supplementary Material).

In order to verify the practical feasibility of CDs, PDS and Pi in real samples were detected using the standard addition method. The same samples of PDS and Pi were also studied by IC with the results listed in Table S5 and Table S6 (in Supplementary Material). It can be found that the recovery of various samples ranges from 98.51 % to 100.13 %. Furthermore, the results from the proposed method and IC are consistent with each other. The data show a very small difference between the added and found content, which can be deduced that the influence of matrix effect is.

thus excluded. It can be deduced that o-CDs and m-CDs can be used for the determination of PDS and Pi in real samples with high accuracy. t-test measurement is also used to evaluate whether the difference is significant in results between the present method and IC (Table S5 and Table S6, in Supplementary Material), it can be found that the values are less than 0.05, which indicates that the measurement uncertainty of the proposed method is low. Moreover, there is good precision and accuracy for the detection of PDS and Pi in food samples.

The quenching effect of PDS and Pi on the fluorescence of o-CDs and m-CDs was the results of both aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and static quenching. First, from TEM images, o-CDs + PDS and m-CDs + Pi show obvious aggregation (Fig. S2, in Supplementary Material), which are consistent with the result of previous work (Chen et al., 2020). The aggregation phenomenon was further proved by DLS (Fig. S5, in Supplementary Material). Thus, the fluorescence quenching was possibly resulting from ACQ. Second, Stern-Volmer plots illustrate that the slope of the curve increases as the temperature rises, which are ascribed to the static quenching mode (Fig. S6, in Supplementary Material). The static mode was based on the formation of ground complexes between excited state of CDs and PDS or Pi. The findings align with the conclusions drawn from previous studies (Amiri et al., 2020; Edison et al). For further confirmation, the fluorescence lifetime of o-CDs and o-CDs + PDS was measured, in which the value of fluorescence lifetime of o-CDs only decreased by 0.01 ns when PDS was added (Fig. S7A, in Supplementary Material). Similarly, the fluorescence lifetime of m-CDs was reduced by 0.02 ns after the addition of Pi (Fig. S7B, in Supplementary Material). The result of τ1/τ2 is close to 1, which is same as the report of Zhang et al. (Zhang et al., 2022) and is much lower than 1.1 reported by Zhao et al. (dynamic quenching) (Zhao et al., 2019). Finally, the combination of ACQ effect and static quenching leads to the fluorescence quenching. The color change of o-CDs and m-CDs was attributed to the formation of o-CDs + PDS and m-CDs + Pi complexes. The specific recognition of PDS and Pi was realized based on the fluorescence quenching and color change based on o-CDs and m-CDs.

In summary, the dual-channel fluorometry and colorimetry were constructed for the determination of PDS and Pi in food samples with good specificity, high sensitivity and portability based on CDs. o/m-CDs were successfully synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method using 5-ASA and OPD/MPD as precursors. On one hand, the fluorometric assay of PDS and Pi was achieved based on the quenching effect of PDS and Pi on the bright green emission of o-CDs and m-CDs, respectively. On the other hand, the colorimetric analysis of PDS and Pi was realized owing to the color change of o-CDs and m-CDs dispersion, respectively. Subsequently, a portable paper-based test strip was developed for quantitative detection of PDS/Pi based on color information from an applet named Colorimetric Detector on smartphone. This approach was effectively applied to detect PDS/Pi in real samples such as instant noodles, bread and potable water. Furthermore, the possible mechanism was explored for the interaction between PDS/Pi and o-CDs/m-CDs, which was ascribed to the formation of complexes. Comparing with previous methods, the present technique enhanced versatility in detection, provided valuable insights for intelligent, portable and timely monitoring of food additives. The strategy showed the significant potential for the detection of PDS and Pi in food samples.

This work was supported financially by China Scholarship Council program (202208370086), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022MB052), Graduate Education Quality Improvement Plan of Shandong Province (SDYJG21198) and research foundation of Liaocheng University (318050022 and 318012116).

Dou Yang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tong Shao: Visualization, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Likai Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Xiaoshuang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qiaoli Yue: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.142905.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots doped by nitrogen and sulfur for dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric determination of Fe3+ and histidine and intracellular imaging of Fe3+ in living cells
Microchimica Acta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted green synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots from mexican mint extract for Fe3+ detection and bio-imaging applications
